steps.title: Bell curves and standardized thinking
steps.body: Normal models describe many real-world measurements. You'll standardize values with z-scores, find areas under the curve, use the empirical rule, and apply the Central Limit Theorem to sample means.

definitions.definition: A symmetric, bell-shaped model centered at the mean.
About 68%, 95%, and 99.7% of data fall within 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations of the mean.
Standardized distance from the mean in standard deviations: z = (x − μ) ÷ σ.
The proportion of values in a region equals the area beneath the curve there.
For large samples, the distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal.
The standard deviation of the sample mean: SE = σ ÷ √n.

steps.title: Empirical rule checkpoint
steps.context: For a roughly normal distribution, recall the 68-95-99.7 rule.
steps.prompt: Shade the region between z = -1 and z = +1 to match the 68% rule.
hints: The first number in 68-95-99.7 corresponds to +/-1 SD.

solution.body: About 68% of values lie between mu - sigma and mu + sigma.
solution.takeaway: The empirical rule gives fast probability approximations for normal data.

steps.title: Compute a z-score
steps.context: Test scores are normal with mean 100 and SD 15.
steps.prompt: What is the z-score for a score of 130?

solution.takeaway: A z-score reports how many SDs a value sits from the mean.

steps.title: Convert z back to raw value
steps.context: Reaction times are normal with mean 70 ms and SD 8 ms.

solution.takeaway: You can move between raw values and z-scores in either direction.

steps.title: Normal shading explorer
steps.prompt: Move the left and right z-bounds to shade probability regions and see area update instantly.

feedback.ready: Areas under the normal curve are probabilities, and z-bounds define those regions.

steps.title: Left-tail area
steps.prompt: Which value is closest to P(Z < 1.2)?
hints: A positive z should have left-tail area greater than 0.5.
Standard normal table gives about 0.8849 at z = 1.2.

feedback.wrong: Check whether the area should be above or below 0.5 before choosing.

solution.takeaway: Sign and size of z should guide quick sanity checks on table values.

steps.title: Middle area between bounds
steps.prompt: Shade the middle region P(-1 < Z < 1) on the standard normal curve.
hints: This is the +/-1 SD interval for the standard normal.
You can also compute Phi(1) - Phi(-1).

feedback.wrong: Use the +/-1 SD area for a standard normal curve.

solution.takeaway: Between-bounds probabilities come from subtraction of cumulative areas.

steps.title: Standard error of the sample mean
steps.context: Population SD is 12 and sample size is n = 36.
steps.prompt: What is the standard error of x-bar?
hints: Standard error for sample mean is sigma/sqrt(n).

solution.takeaway: Sampling distributions narrow as n grows because SE shrinks like 1/sqrt(n).

steps.title: Sample-mean tail probability
steps.context: Population has mu = 100, sigma = 12. For samples of n = 36, consider x-bar.
hints: Right tail above z = 1.5 is about 0.0668.

feedback.wrong: Standardize x-bar first, then use a right-tail normal area.

solution.takeaway: Sample-mean probabilities use SE, not the raw population SD.

steps.title: Z-score for an individual score
steps.context: Final exam scores are approximately normal with mu = 78 and sigma = 10.

solution.takeaway: Capstone starts by translating raw values into the standard normal scale.

steps.title: Upper-tail percentage
steps.context: Same exam model: mu = 78, sigma = 10. From part (a), z = 1.4.
hints: Right-tail area above z = 1.4 is about 0.0808.

feedback.wrong: Use right-tail area after converting to z = 1.4.

solution.takeaway: Table values become actionable percentages once you identify the right tail.

steps.title: Sample mean probability
steps.context: Now take samples of n = 25 from the same population (mu = 78, sigma = 10).
hints: Right-tail area above z = 2 is about 0.0228.

feedback.wrong: Use the sample-mean SE and then find the right-tail z area.

solution.takeaway: Capstone thread: standardize correctly for individuals vs sample means.

steps.title: Chapter 5 complete
steps.body:
You connected normal models, z-scores, shaded areas, and sampling distributions to answer individual and sample-mean probability questions.

Next up: estimation with confidence intervals, margin of error, and sample-size planning.
